source.messages:
Rafael Mendes: gameday debrief thread (status page focus). drop notes + gaps + action items here so we can roll them into the runbook + bot tweaks.

incident-bot: Drill concluded: "Partial streaming degradation (us-east)". Duration 37m. Public updates posted: 2. Components updated: 3. :clipboard:

Christine Wong: overall: good improvement vs last drill on TTFU, but we still stumbled on (a) verifying impacted components before posting, and (b) anti-flap behavior causing a bunch of “degraded/operational/degraded” toggles in staging.

Allison Grant: +1. the anti-flap hysteresis isn’t being applied consistently across the new component signals. some panels are using raw 1m rates and others are smoothed. so depending on which dashboard folks check, they see different stories.

Aisha Bello: the oncall flow today was basically:
1) see paging alert (burn)
2) open dashboard A (gateway errors) -> looks bad
3) open dashboard B (streaming disconnects) -> looks noisy / not sure if real
4) decide to mark “Hosted API - us-east” degraded to be safe
…then later we realized chat was fine, streaming was the only real impact.

Mina Haddad: we also had confusion on region scope. the drill injected ~70% of the errors in us-east, but eu-west had a smaller spike due to fallback traffic. we debated whether to mark both regions. I don’t think we have a clear rule for “secondary region overloaded due to failover traffic”.

Devon Price: from the incident-bot side: the bot asked for component + region, but didn’t force “capability” (streaming vs chat vs embeddings). so people defaulted to “Hosted API” because it’s the top-level thing.

Rafael Mendes: yep. we should make capability required for Hosted API components unless it’s truly broad. today’s drill showed that “Hosted API (us-east)” is still too coarse for comms.

Sean Gallagher: key goal for this initiative: fewer “we marked the wrong thing because we couldn’t verify quickly.” if verification is hard, the taxonomy doesn’t matter. we need a minimum verification checklist that’s fast.

Christine Wong: proposed checklist (for Comms Lead / IC) before first public update:
- confirm region(s): check routing dashboard (traffic shift) + per-region error panel
- confirm capability: at least one of (streaming disconnects), (chat 5xx), (embeddings 5xx), (rerank 5xx)
- confirm customer impact signal: elevated error% OR p95 latency > threshold for 10m (not 1m)
- confirm it’s not a single customer route (look at top routes / route cardinality)

Allison Grant: re: anti-flap: we need to stop using 1m spikes to drive status. for customer-facing, I’d suggest:
- degrade only if sustained for 10m (or 2/3 of last 15m)
- recover only if sustained normal for 15m
- and use separate thresholds for "degraded" vs "partial outage" (error-based vs latency-based)

Aisha Bello: + add a hard rule: no more than 1 status flip per component per 15m unless IC overrides. today staging auto-signal flipped 4x in 12m.

Dev Patel: from Support POV, flip-flopping is brutal. customers screenshot the status page and then we get “why did it go back to operational while we still see failures” even if it’s just threshold noise.

Miles Thornton: I watched the drill. we also lost time because folks weren’t sure where “source of truth” is for component mapping + which dashboard corresponds to which component. someone pasted an old panel link.

Rafael Mendes: action: in the runbook we should have a single “Status component → dashboard link” table. no hunting. (I know we have a spreadsheet but it’s not in the runbook path.)

Christine Wong: +1. I’ll add to the runbook: “component verification links” and make it the default section people land on.

Allison Grant: also, can we standardize the panel titles to match public component names? right now it’s “gateway-us-east” and “stream-sse” etc. people don’t map that mentally during a Sev.

Aisha Bello: I can take that. I’ll update dashboard naming + add a “public_component_id” label in the header / description.

Devon Price: for bot: I can add a step:
- “Select impacted capability: Chat / Streaming / Embeddings / Reranking / Console / Dedicated control plane”
- then bot suggests components accordingly (ex: Streaming → “Hosted API Streaming (us-east)” etc)
Also can add a prompt: “Have you checked the component verification dashboard? yes/no” (soft block).

Rafael Mendes: please make it a soft block. we don’t want the bot to slow down Sev0.

Sean Gallagher: agree. soft block + reminder + link to runbook.

Mina Haddad: on the region scope question (secondary region overloaded): can we encode a rule like “if impact is due to failover, mark the impacted region(s) where customers observe errors/latency, not the origin region”? But we need wording guidance so we don’t say “capacity issue”.

Christine Wong: maybe in templates: “We are seeing elevated error rates for Streaming requests in us-east. Some customers may also see increased latency in eu-west due to traffic shifts while we mitigate.” That avoids “capacity”.

Dev Patel: that wording is workable. Support can mirror it without getting into internals.

Allison Grant: technical note: today’s flip-flop happened because the “degraded” signal used p95 latency (5m) but the “recovered” check used error% (1m). totally inconsistent.

Aisha Bello: that’s on me. the signal definitions drifted. we should codify in the customer-facing signals standard: windows + required metrics for each capability.

Rafael Mendes: action items (draft):
1) Runbook: add verification checklist + component→dashboard links (Christine)
2) Observability: align panel naming + standardize windows + apply hysteresis consistently (Allison/Aisha)
3) incident-bot: require capability selection + suggest component(s) + soft verify prompt (Devon)
4) Policy: clarify “secondary region due to failover” guidance + approved wording (Christine + Dev Patel + Kira later)

Miles Thornton: one more: add a “manual override” note in runbook for when the auto-signal is flapping. today folks weren’t sure if they were allowed to pin degraded while monitoring.

Rafael Mendes: good catch. yes, add “pin status” guidance + when to do it + who approves.

Sean Gallagher: and please capture metric: time-to-first-update in the drill (from declare to first status post). if we’re adding steps, we need to ensure we still hit target.

incident-bot: Drill metrics summary:
- Declared incident → first internal update: 3m
- Declared incident → first status page update: 11m
- Status page updates posted: Investigating, Monitoring
- Status flips detected in auto-signal (staging): 4

Christine Wong: 11m is fine, but we can do better once the bot suggests the right component. most of the time was spent debating scope.

Allison Grant: I’ll open a follow-up to make anti-flap a library used by all component signals, not copy/pasted logic per panel.

Devon Price: I’ll prototype the capability prompt tomorrow and post screenshots in #eng-sre for review.

Rafael Mendes: thanks all. I’ll move these into the Linear ticket and link back here. if you remember any specific "wait why are we checking this" moments, reply w/ the exact step + what link you wish you had.

Dev Patel: one specific: when we posted “Monitoring” we didn’t say what changed. customers always ask “what did you do.” maybe template should include a safe line like “We applied mitigations to reduce disconnect rates and are monitoring recovery.”

Christine Wong: +1, will add to Monitoring block: include what changed (at a high level) + what we’re watching next.

Sean Gallagher: closing thought: we should prioritize reducing incorrect component selection over adding more copy. wrong component = instant trust hit.

Rafael Mendes: agreed. will treat “capability required + suggested component” as P0.

Rafael Mendes: yep. we should make capability required for Hosted API components unless it’s truly broad. today’s drill showed that “Hosted API (us-east)” is still too coarse for comms.

Sean Gallagher: key goal for this initiative: fewer “we marked the wrong thing because we couldn’t verify quickly.” if verification is hard, the taxonomy doesn’t matter. we need a minimum verification checklist that’s fast.

Christine Wong: proposed checklist (for Comms Lead / IC) before first public update:
- confirm region(s): check routing dashboard (traffic shift) + per-region error panel
- confirm capability: at least one of (streaming disconnects), (chat 5xx), (embeddings 5xx), (rerank 5xx)
- confirm customer impact signal: elevated error% OR p95 latency > threshold for 10m (not 1m)
- confirm it’s not a single customer route (look at top routes / route cardinality)

Allison Grant: re: anti-flap: we need to stop using 1m spikes to drive status. for customer-facing, I’d suggest:
- degrade only if sustained for 10m (or 2/3 of last 15m)
- recover only if sustained normal for 15m
- and use separate thresholds for "degraded" vs "partial outage" (error-based vs latency-based)

Aisha Bello: + add a hard rule: no more than 1 status flip per component per 15m unless IC overrides. today staging auto-signal flipped 4x in 12m.

Dev Patel: from Support POV, flip-flopping is brutal. customers screenshot the status page and then we get “why did it go back to operational while we still see failures” even if it’s just threshold noise.

Miles Thornton: I watched the drill. we also lost time because folks weren’t sure where “source of truth” is for component mapping + which dashboard corresponds to which component. someone pasted an old panel link.

Rafael Mendes: action: in the runbook we should have a single “Status component → dashboard link” table. no hunting. (I know we have a spreadsheet but it’s not in the runbook path.)

Christine Wong: +1. I’ll add to the runbook: “component verification links” and make it the default section people land on.

Allison Grant: also, can we standardize the panel titles to match public component names? right now it’s “gateway-us-east” and “stream-sse” etc. people don’t map that mentally during a Sev.

Aisha Bello: I can take that. I’ll update dashboard naming + add a “public_component_id” label in the header / description.

Devon Price: for bot: I can add a step:
- “Select impacted capability: Chat / Streaming / Embeddings / Reranking / Console / Dedicated control plane”
- then bot suggests components accordingly (ex: Streaming → “Hosted API Streaming (us-east)” etc)
Also can add a prompt: “Have you checked the component verification dashboard? yes/no” (soft block).

Rafael Mendes: please make it a soft block. we don’t want the bot to slow down Sev0.

Sean Gallagher: agree. soft block + reminder + link to runbook.

Mina Haddad: on the region scope question (secondary region overloaded): can we encode a rule like “if impact is due to failover, mark the impacted region(s) where customers observe errors/latency, not the origin region”? But we need wording guidance so we don’t say “capacity issue”.

Christine Wong: maybe in templates: “We are seeing elevated error rates for Streaming requests in us-east. Some customers may also see increased latency in eu-west due to traffic shifts while we mitigate.” That avoids “capacity”.

Dev Patel: that wording is workable. Support can mirror it without getting into internals.

Allison Grant: technical note: today’s flip-flop happened because the “degraded” signal used p95 latency (5m) but the “recovered” check used error% (1m). totally inconsistent.

Aisha Bello: that’s on me. the signal definitions drifted. we should codify in the customer-facing signals standard: windows + required metrics for each capability.

Rafael Mendes: action items (draft):
1) Runbook: add verification checklist + component→dashboard links (Christine)
2) Observability: align panel naming + standardize windows + apply hysteresis consistently (Allison/Aisha)
3) incident-bot: require capability selection + suggest component(s) + soft verify prompt (Devon)
4) Policy: clarify “secondary region due to failover” guidance + approved wording (Christine + Dev Patel + Kira later)

Miles Thornton: one more: add a “manual override” note in runbook for when the auto-signal is flapping. today folks weren’t sure if they were allowed to pin degraded while monitoring.

Rafael Mendes: good catch. yes, add “pin status” guidance + when to do it + who approves.

Sean Gallagher: and please capture metric: time-to-first-update in the drill (from declare to first status post). if we’re adding steps, we need to ensure we still hit target.

incident-bot: Drill metrics summary:
- Declared incident → first internal update: 3m
- Declared incident → first status page update: 11m
- Status page updates posted: Investigating, Monitoring
- Status flips detected in auto-signal (staging): 4

Christine Wong: 11m is fine, but we can do better once the bot suggests the right component. most of the time was spent debating scope.

Allison Grant: I’ll open a follow-up to make anti-flap a library used by all component signals, not copy/pasted logic per panel.

Devon Price: I’ll prototype the capability prompt tomorrow and post screenshots in #eng-sre for review.

Rafael Mendes: thanks all. I’ll move these into the Linear ticket and link back here. if you remember any specific "wait why are we checking this" moments, reply w/ the exact step + what link you wish you had.

Dev Patel: one specific: when we posted “Monitoring” we didn’t say what changed. customers always ask “what did you do.” maybe template should include a safe line like “We applied mitigations to reduce disconnect rates and are monitoring recovery.”

Christine Wong: +1, will add to Monitoring block: include what changed (at a high level) + what we’re watching next.

Sean Gallagher: closing thought: we should prioritize reducing incorrect component selection over adding more copy. wrong component = instant trust hit.

Rafael Mendes: agreed. will treat “capability required + suggested component” as P0.

source.content:
Dev Patel: heads up post-incident feedback coming in already — multiple customers saying status page was too coarse. Quotes: “status page says API ok but embeddings failing” and “we only saw 'Hosted API' but it was streaming disconnects in us-east.” :/ We need to log concrete follow-ups.

Rachel Kim: +1. I have 3 enterprise threads open. One (Northbridge) literally asked if we can show per-region + capability. They were watching the status page and couldn’t tell it was limited to streaming.

Jasmine Liu: same from CS side. Customers are comparing their errors vs our status updates and concluding we’re hiding things. It’s more that our components are too broad.

incident-bot: Incident 2025-11-07 marked Resolved. Reminder: post-incident action items can be captured with `/incident actions`.

Rafael Mendes: yeah, this is exactly the gap we’ve been talking about. The public components are still basically “Hosted API” and “Console” and it breaks down for partial feature failures.

Rafael Mendes: can we list out the specific complaints so we don’t lose detail?

Dev Patel: from Support tickets + emails today:
- “Embeddings endpoint 500s for ~20m but status page green” (customer included request IDs)
- “Streaming responses disconnecting; non-streaming fine; status says Hosted API degraded which is too broad”
- “Impact only in us-east but status update didn’t mention region; they thought all regions impacted”

Rachel Kim: also: one customer said they didn’t know if Dedicated capacity was impacted vs Hosted. They saw “API issues” and assumed Dedicated too.

Sean Gallagher: this is a comms + taxonomy issue, not just a support issue. We should treat this as a clear action item for the status page transparency upgrades work.

Christine Wong: agree. we need component granularity by product surface + region at minimum. capability split too (streaming vs non-streaming) where signals exist.

Allison Grant: on the signals side, we *can* separate streaming disconnect/errors now (at least in us-east/us-west). embeddings vs chat is messier because gateway errors roll up unless we tag routes consistently.

Ravi Menon: route tagging is the root of it. current component mapping is basically “all /v1/* = hosted api”. We can map by route group:
- /v1/chat/completions (non-streaming)
- /v1/chat/completions?stream=true (streaming)
- /v1/embeddings
- /v1/rerank
but only if the metrics include route/capability tags.

Mina Haddad: region dimension is doable. Our edge/gateway already tags region, but status page components don’t reflect it. We can create: “Hosted API (us-east)”, “Streaming (us-east)”, etc and group visually.

Nora Ahmed: from Console POV, if we publish more granular components, we can link incidents to the impacted capability + region in-console. But we need stable IDs + copy guidance (don’t accidentally imply SLA per component).

Sean Gallagher: +1. keep it factual: what is impacted, which regions, what mitigations, and what’s known/unknown. No promises.

Dev Patel: what’s the fastest immediate mitigation for Support? even before the full redesign.

Rafael Mendes: short term:
1) update incident templates to always include region + affected capability when known
2) ensure Comms Lead asks “is this streaming-only?” early
3) add a Support macro: “Status page components are currently coarse; we’ll specify impacted endpoints/regions in updates” (but ideally we fix the components quickly)

Jasmine Liu: I’m ok with a macro as a stopgap but we should set expectation we’re shipping the component split soon. Customers hate “coming soon” language without dates.

Christine Wong: action items I’m hearing:
- define component taxonomy: Hosted API vs Streaming vs Embeddings vs Reranking + split by region
- map internal signals to these components + anti-flap so we don’t toggle constantly
- update incident-bot intake to require component/region/capability
- create dashboards per component so on-call can validate quickly

Allison Grant: + add a weekly “status accuracy audit” job comparing incident tags vs status updates. We keep repeating the same mismatch patterns.

Ravi Menon: also need tests on the mapping layer so we don’t misclassify embeddings as chat again.

Dev Patel: can we capture this in Linear with owners? I’ll link the support tickets but need eng to take the mapping work.

Rafael Mendes: yep. I’ll open/attach to:
- ENG-4821 Status page component granularity (owner Christine)
- ENG-4830 Map internal services to status components (owner Ravi)
- ENG-4822 Incident-bot statuspage integration (owner Devon)
- ENG-4841 Add component-level SLO burn alerts (owner Aisha)

Sean Gallagher: make sure Support/CS are in the review loop for wording + what gets exposed. Also: Dedicated/Private messaging needs guardrails.

Jasmine Liu: please include me + Rachel on the template review. We need “what to say when customers dispute accuracy” as part of the process.

Dev Patel: I’ll link the concrete tickets:
- SUP-28411 status page missing embeddings component
- SUP-28549 streaming disconnects not reflected on status
- SUP-28436 disputed status accuracy during us-east degradation

Christine Wong: cool. I’ll draft the component list + definitions for review in #eng-sre this week.

Nora Ahmed: if we add region-capability components, pls coordinate naming/grouping w/ design so the page isn’t unreadable on mobile.

Allison Grant: I’ll start a quick doc with proposed signals/thresholds per component + anti-flap windows. We should avoid “latency-only degraded” until we agree on criteria.

Rafael Mendes: sounds good. let’s treat this thread as the “customer pain evidence” for the project brief.

Sean Gallagher: :thumbsup: thanks all. goal is fewer debates mid-incident about “what do we mark”. taxonomy + tooling should make it obvious.

Dev Patel: 👍 I’ll update the incident follow-up email chain with: we heard the feedback + we’re rolling out more granular components + improved post-incident summaries (once approved).

Rachel Kim: +1. and please make sure we include an ETA only when we’re confident. For now we can say “in progress” and we’ll notify once live.

Rafael Mendes: closing loop: action items logged in Linear; next check-in in SRE weekly. any other customer quotes/screenshots drop here.

Jasmine Liu: adding one more quote from a QBR note: “We need to know if it’s embeddings vs chat vs streaming; otherwise we can’t decide whether to fail over.”

Dev Patel: yup, that’s the one. pinning this thread for now.

incident-bot: Action items recorded: component granularity, incident template updates, mapping + dashboards, support macro alignment.

incident-bot: :rotating_light: Sev1 declared by @Rafael Mendes — "Streaming disconnects / partial degradation" 
War room: #inc-1760048421
IC: @Rafael Mendes
Scribe: @Christine Wong
Comms: (unassigned)

Rafael Mendes: seeing elevated client disconnects on streaming responses. starting triage. can someone pull region breakdown + confirm if non-streaming impacted?

Allison Grant: on it. dashboards:
- Gateway errors (all): https://grafana.redwood.dev/d/gw-overview
- Streaming disconnects (by region): https://grafana.redwood.dev/d/streaming-health?var-region=all
- p95 latency (chat non-stream): https://grafana.redwood.dev/d/chat-latency
initial glance: spike looks concentrated in us-east.

Aisha Bello: SLO burn is tripping for "hosted_api.streaming.us-east" (fast burn window). not seeing burn on "hosted_api.chat.us-east" (non-stream). error rate is noisy but disconnect counter is clearly up.

Christine Wong: who’s comms lead? i can take if needed, but I’m also scribing.

Rafael Mendes: @Christine Wong please take Comms Lead for now. I’ll keep IC.

incident-bot: Comms Lead set to @Christine Wong. Reminder: target first public update within 15 minutes for Sev1.

Mina Haddad: fyi routing: us-east is primary for a bunch of accounts but we have spillover to us-west on some tiers. if we mark component by region, likely "US East" only unless we confirm spillover degradation.

Logan Wright: checking metrics tags — streaming disconnects labeled `capability=streaming` and `region=us-east` are ~6-7x baseline starting ~10 min ago. us-west steady.
also seeing elevated `upstream_reset` on the streaming gateway path only.

Ethan Park: control-plane looks fine (no scheduler churn). this seems dataplane / gateway-to-runtime behavior.

Devon Price: I can check incident-bot statuspage integration fields. what component are we thinking? we now have:
- Hosted API (US East) — Chat Completions
- Hosted API (US East) — Streaming
- Hosted API (US East) — Embeddings
etc

Christine Wong: question for IC/obs: do we mark "Hosted API - US East - Streaming" as degraded now, or wait until we validate customer impact threshold? support will get pinged either way.

Allison Grant: we have clear customer-facing symptom: disconnects mid-stream. looks like partial (not total outage). I’d say "Degraded Performance" for streaming in us-east.

Rafael Mendes: agree it’s real, but want to avoid over-scoping. does it affect all streaming endpoints or only chat completions with `stream=true`? what about `/v1/responses` streaming?

Logan Wright: gateway route tags suggest most hits are `/v1/chat/completions` with `stream=true`. `/v1/responses` volume is smaller but shows similar pattern (disconnect ratio higher than baseline). not sure if same cause.

Aisha Bello: impact is not 100% failures — more like increased early disconnects + some elevated 5xx when clients retry aggressively. latency dashboard for non-stream looks ok.

Dev Patel: support is already seeing 3 tickets mentioning “stream cuts out” and “events stop after ~20s” (no mention of embeddings). they’re all us-east customers.

Irene Choi: we should be precise: "Streaming" component only, region us-east only. If we mark the entire Hosted API us-east as degraded, customers will assume non-streaming is broken.

Christine Wong: ok. draft status update:
"Investigating - We’re investigating increased disconnects affecting streaming responses for Hosted API in US East. Non-streaming requests appear unaffected. We’ll provide an update within 30 minutes."
Thoughts?

Rafael Mendes: good. remove the “appear unaffected” absolute vibe; maybe “We are not currently observing impact to non-streaming requests”

Allison Grant: +1. also avoid committing to 30 min, we can say “We’ll share updates as we learn more.”

Mina Haddad: also, if routing fails over, customers in us-east might get served from us-west. we can mention “some requests may be routed to alternate regions” or is that too much detail?

Dev Patel: prefer not mentioning routing/failover specifics publicly unless it’s relevant. customers mainly need: what is impacted, where, workaround.

Christine Wong: ok revised:
"Investigating - We’re investigating increased disconnects affecting streaming responses for Hosted API in US East. At this time, we are not observing impact to non-streaming requests. We’ll provide another update as we learn more." 

Rafael Mendes: ship it. mark component “Hosted API (US East) — Streaming” as Degraded Performance.

statuspage-bot: Draft update created (phase=Investigating, component=Hosted API (US East) — Streaming, status=Degraded Performance). Awaiting publish.

Devon Price: publishing now unless anyone objects.

Christine Wong: go ahead.

statuspage-bot: :white_check_mark: Update published to status page.
Text: "Investigating - We’re investigating increased disconnects affecting streaming responses for Hosted API in US East. At this time, we are not observing impact to non-streaming requests. We’ll provide another update as we learn more."

Rafael Mendes: working hypothesis: gateway -> runtime connection resets triggered by recent config change? did we roll anything in us-east?

Allison Grant: deploy log shows gateway canary in us-east 20 min ago (feature flag for idle timeout tuning). checking if it reached 100%.

Irene Choi: it was supposed to be 10% only. looking now.

Logan Wright: correlating: spike starts ~3 min after config change. smells like it.

Rafael Mendes: mitigation: roll back the idle timeout change in us-east, hold other regions.

Irene Choi: rolling back now. ETA 5-7 minutes to propagate.

Christine Wong: for status: do we move to "Identified" once we confirm correlation, or wait until rollback takes effect?

Rafael Mendes: let’s wait for confirmation (disconnect ratio drops). then post “Identified” + “Mitigation in progress (rollback).”

Aisha Bello: monitoring burn rate — still burning but flattening a bit. will update when it clearly trends down.

Dev Patel: please keep status scoped. customers are specifically asking if they should disable streaming. what’s our guidance?

Rafael Mendes: we can suggest temporary workaround: retry with non-streaming or shorter max_tokens; but careful. maybe: “If you are able, you can temporarily use non-streaming mode as a workaround.”

Christine Wong: ok. once rollback is in flight, I’ll add a note about non-streaming workaround.

Irene Choi: rollback done. config reverted in us-east gateway. watching metrics.

Allison Grant: disconnect counter is dropping quickly. looks like we’re recovering.

Aisha Bello: SLO burn windows returning to normal range. p95 disconnect ratio back near baseline.

Christine Wong: posting update:
"Identified - We identified a configuration change that increased streaming disconnects for Hosted API in US East. We have rolled back the change and are monitoring recovery. If you’re impacted, temporarily using non-streaming mode may help while recovery completes." 
OK?

Rafael Mendes: yes.

statuspage-bot: :white_check_mark: Update published (phase=Identified).

Mina Haddad: do we need to mark any other components? seeing slight elevated retries but confined. I’d keep only streaming/us-east.

Logan Wright: agree. retries are downstream effect; not enough to justify broader component status.

Devon Price: incident-bot reminds we should verify component/region tags in incident record match statuspage component. currently set: component_id=hosted_api.streaming, region=us-east, capability=streaming.

Christine Wong: :thumbsup: captured.

Dev Patel: support macro update: “Known issue with streaming in us-east, non-streaming ok, workaround non-streaming.” Linking status page incident.

Rafael Mendes: moving to monitoring for 30 min. if stable, we’ll resolve.

Allison Grant: continuing to watch. also filing follow-up to add guardrails on idle-timeout flag.

Christine Wong: will post monitoring message when we have 10-15 min stable.

Aisha Bello: 12 min stable. no resurgence.

Christine Wong: posting Monitoring update.

statuspage-bot: :white_check_mark: Update published (phase=Monitoring).
Text: "Monitoring - The rollback is complete and streaming disconnect rates in US East have returned to normal levels. We’re continuing to monitor to ensure full recovery."

Rafael Mendes: thanks. keep component as degraded until we’re comfortable.

Irene Choi: agree. if stable another ~15 min we can resolve.

Christine Wong: quick question: do we ever mark this as “Partial Outage” vs “Degraded Performance”? we got a lot of disconnects but not total.

Allison Grant: I’d keep Degraded Performance. Partial Outage tends to imply widespread failure; this was elevated disconnects + retries, but many streams succeeded.

Rafael Mendes: +1. resolve when stable.

Aisha Bello: 30 min post-rollback stable. burn rate cleared.

Rafael Mendes: resolving.

statuspage-bot: :white_check_mark: Incident marked Resolved. Component restored to Operational.

Christine Wong: adding final note: “Root cause was a configuration change; rolled back.” and linking internal retro doc once ready (not public yet).

Dev Patel: appreciate the narrow scoping — saved a lot of confusion on tickets.

Rafael Mendes: good work. pls capture the decision rationale (why streaming/us-east only) for the post-incident summary template.

source.messages:
incident-bot: :rotating_light: SEV1 incident declared by @Rafael Mendes
incident-bot: Title: Streaming disconnects / elevated errors (suspected us-east)
incident-bot: Owner: @Rafael Mendes | Scribe: (unassigned) | Comms Lead: (unassigned)
incident-bot: Reminder: customer-facing update target (TTFU) = 15 min for SEV1.

Rafael Mendes: need a comms template pls. we should get a first status update out but i don’t want to over-speculate. can someone drop the “Investigating” block we agreed on?

Christine Wong: yep. before i paste: what are we comfortable saying? we have elevated disconnects on streaming only, right? chat non-streaming looks fine?

Allison Grant: from dashboards: `streaming_disconnect_rate` spiking us-east-1, starting ~7 mins ago. errors mostly client disconnect / upstream reset. p95 latency on non-streaming stable.

Rafael Mendes: ok. customer impact: some % of streaming requests failing / terminating early in us-east. not sure if all tenants.

Wei Chen: for Investigating we should avoid % unless we’re confident + avoid “all customers”. keep it “some customers may see…” and “we’re investigating elevated errors”.

Dev Patel: +1. Support is already getting “my streams drop” pings. status page should say *streaming* and *us-east* if we’re reasonably sure. helps deflect.

Miles Thornton: reminder on timing: it’s better to post “Investigating” with scoped component + region than wait for full RCA. also add “next update in X minutes” so people don’t refresh spam.

Rafael Mendes: cool. can we craft: “We are investigating reports of elevated errors for streaming responses in US-East. Non-streaming requests unaffected.” is that too strong?

Allison Grant: “non-streaming unaffected” feels ok based on current signals, but maybe phrase as “we have not observed impact to…”

Christine Wong: ok pasting a template we’ve been using + tweak for this incident:

Christine Wong: Draft v1:
"Investigating — We are investigating an issue causing elevated error rates for Streaming responses in the US-East region. Some requests may disconnect or terminate early. We have not observed impact to non-streaming requests at this time. Next update in 15 minutes."

Wei Chen: looks good. tiny tweaks:
- “US East” vs “US-East” (status page usually uses “US East (N. Virginia)”)
- remove “some requests may…”? i think it’s fine actually.

Dev Patel: keep “disconnect or terminate early” — that matches what customers report. but avoid “may” twice. maybe:
"Some streaming requests are disconnecting or terminating early."

Rafael Mendes: ok. can we make it less absolute on region? we *think* us-east but might be edge proxy. maybe: “primarily affecting US East”?

Allison Grant: it’s cleanly in us-east graphs, but CDN edges could make it look global for certain customers. “primarily affecting US East” seems safe.

Devon Price: btw incident-bot can auto-fill the status update fields if you tell me the component + region. are we marking component as “Hosted API - Streaming (US East)” or still the old “API” bucket? (status page is still coarse in prod)

Christine Wong: status page currently has “API” + “Dashboard” only. but we can mention streaming in the message body even if the component is “API”.

Rafael Mendes: ok. set component=API (for now), message includes streaming + region.

Wei Chen: propose final wording:
"Investigating — We are investigating elevated error rates affecting streaming responses, primarily in the US East region. Some streaming requests are disconnecting or terminating early. We have not observed impact to non-streaming requests at this time. Next update in 15 minutes."

Dev Patel: +1. also: keep it short. statuspage truncates on mobile.

Miles Thornton: agree. also, if we’re going to post “next update in 15” we need a reminder timer internally.

incident-bot: :bell: TTFU reminder: 8 minutes elapsed since SEV1 declared. Customer-facing update due in 7 minutes.

Rafael Mendes: thanks. @Christine Wong can you post that wording? i’ll approve.

Christine Wong: yep. posting now unless any objections.

Allison Grant: no objections.

Rafael Mendes: approved.

Christine Wong: Statuspage update (Investigating) posted:
"Investigating — We are investigating elevated error rates affecting streaming responses, primarily in the US East region. Some streaming requests are disconnecting or terminating early. We have not observed impact to non-streaming requests at this time. Next update in 15 minutes."

Dev Patel: :thumbsup: linking this in the support macro.

Wei Chen: nice. when we move to Identified, we should avoid “root cause” unless we’re sure. we can say “identified contributing factor” if needed.

Rafael Mendes: agreed. next internal checkpoint in 10, public in 15.

incident-bot: :memo: Comms log updated by @Christine Wong. Next update target: 15 minutes.

title: #incidents: 1760049133

Dev Patel: +1. also: keep it short. statuspage truncates on mobile.

Miles Thornton: agree. also, if we’re going to post “next update in 15” we need a reminder timer internally.

incident-bot: :bell: TTFU reminder: 8 minutes elapsed since SEV1 declared. Customer-facing update due in 7 minutes.

Rafael Mendes: thanks. @Christine Wong can you post that wording? i’ll approve.

Christine Wong: yep. posting now unless any objections.

Allison Grant: no objections.

Rafael Mendes: approved.

Christine Wong: Statuspage update (Investigating) posted:
"Investigating — We are investigating elevated error rates affecting streaming responses, primarily in the US East region. Some streaming requests are disconnecting or terminating early. We have not observed impact to non-streaming requests at this time. Next update in 15 minutes."

Dev Patel: :thumbsup: linking this in the support macro.

Wei Chen: nice. when we move to Identified, we should avoid “root cause” unless we’re sure. we can say “identified contributing factor” if needed.

Rafael Mendes: agreed. next internal checkpoint in 10, public in 15.

incident-bot: :memo: Comms log updated by @Christine Wong. Next update target: 15 minutes.
text:
incident-bot: :rotating_light: SEV1 incident declared by @Rafael Mendes
incident-bot: Title: Streaming disconnects / elevated errors (suspected us-east)
incident-bot: Owner: @Rafael Mendes | Scribe: (unassigned) | Comms Lead: (unassigned)
incident-bot: Reminder: customer-facing update target (TTFU) = 15 min for SEV1.

Rafael Mendes: need a comms template pls. we should get a first status update out but i don’t want to over-speculate. can someone drop the “Investigating” block we agreed on?

Christine Wong: yep. before i paste: what are we comfortable saying? we have elevated disconnects on streaming only, right? chat non-streaming looks fine?

Allison Grant: from dashboards: `streaming_disconnect_rate` spiking us-east-1, starting ~7 mins ago. errors mostly client disconnect / upstream reset. p95 latency on non-streaming stable.

Rafael Mendes: ok. customer impact: some % of streaming requests failing / terminating early in us-east. not sure if all tenants.

Wei Chen: for Investigating we should avoid % unless we’re confident + avoid “all customers”. keep it “some customers may see…” and “we’re investigating elevated errors”.

Dev Patel: +1. Support is already getting “my streams drop” pings. status page should say *streaming* and *us-east* if we’re reasonably sure. helps deflect.

Miles Thornton: reminder on timing: it’s better to post “Investigating” with scoped component + region than wait for full RCA. also add “next update in X minutes” so people don’t refresh spam.

Rafael Mendes: cool. can we craft: “We are investigating reports of elevated errors for streaming responses in US-East. Non-streaming requests unaffected.” is that too strong?

Allison Grant: “non-streaming unaffected” feels ok based on current signals, but maybe phrase as “we have not observed impact to…”

Christine Wong: ok pasting a template we’ve been using + tweak for this incident:

Christine Wong: Draft v1:
"Investigating — We are investigating an issue causing elevated error rates for Streaming responses in the US-East region. Some requests may disconnect or terminate early. We have not observed impact to non-streaming requests at this time. Next update in 15 minutes."

Wei Chen: looks good. tiny tweaks:
- “US East” vs “US-East” (status page usually uses “US East (N. Virginia)”)
- remove “some requests may…”? i think it’s fine actually.

Dev Patel: keep “disconnect or terminate early” — that matches what customers report. but avoid “may” twice. maybe:
"Some streaming requests are disconnecting or terminating early."

Rafael Mendes: ok. can we make it less absolute on region? we *think* us-east but might be edge proxy. maybe: “primarily affecting US East”?

Allison Grant: it’s cleanly in us-east graphs, but CDN edges could make it look global for certain customers. “primarily affecting US East” seems safe.

Devon Price: btw incident-bot can auto-fill the status update fields if you tell me the component + region. are we marking component as “Hosted API - Streaming (US East)” or still the old “API” bucket? (status page is still coarse in prod)

Christine Wong: status page currently has “API” + “Dashboard” only. but we can mention streaming in the message body even if the component is “API”.

Rafael Mendes: ok. set component=API (for now), message includes streaming + region.

Wei Chen: propose final wording:
"Investigating — We are investigating elevated error rates affecting streaming responses, primarily in the US East region. Some streaming requests are disconnecting or terminating early. We have not observed impact to non-streaming requests at this time. Next update in 15 minutes."

Dev Patel: +1. also: keep it short. statuspage truncates on mobile.

Miles Thornton: agree. also, if we’re going to post “next update in 15” we need a reminder timer internally.

incident-bot: :bell: TTFU reminder: 8 minutes elapsed since SEV1 declared. Customer-facing update due in 7 minutes.

Rafael Mendes: thanks. @Christine Wong can you post that wording? i’ll approve.

Christine Wong: yep. posting now unless any objections.

Allison Grant: no objections.

Rafael Mendes: approved.

Christine Wong: Statuspage update (Investigating) posted:
"Investigating — We are investigating elevated error rates affecting streaming responses, primarily in the US East region. Some streaming requests are disconnecting or terminating early. We have not observed impact to non-streaming requests at this time. Next update in 15 minutes."

Dev Patel: :thumbsup: linking this in the support macro.

Wei Chen: nice. when we move to Identified, we should avoid “root cause” unless we’re sure. we can say “identified contributing factor” if needed.

Rafael Mendes: agreed. next internal checkpoint in 10, public in 15.

incident-bot: :memo: Comms log updated by @Christine Wong. Next update target: 15 minutes.

incident-bot: Incident declared: INC-2025-07-18-02 Sev2 "Hosted API streaming disconnects". Channel created; assign IC/Ops/Comms/SME.
Grace O'Connor: Im IC. Ops: @Ethan Park. SME: @Hiro Tanaka + @Rohan Mehta. Comms: @Vanessa Ortiz.
Hiro Tanaka: seeing spikes in `stream_disconnect` + restarts in us-east runtime pods. suspect experimental scheduler under high concurrency again.
Rohan Mehta: agree. mitigation: force `RW_SCHED_EXPERIMENTAL=0` and/or ship the gate hotfix. rollback tag is runtime-2.18.1.
Ethan Park: I can do config flip now as Tier0 while Hiro cuts PR. Need EPC approvals per policy.
Grace O'Connor: approved (IC). Need runtime approver too.
Rohan Mehta: approved from runtime side. do canary cell first.
Ethan Park: deploying config change to canary cell us-east; watching 5xx/latency/disconnects.
Vanessa Ortiz: drafting status update; can you confirm customer-visible?
Grace O'Connor: yes, subset of streaming requests affected; post degraded performance + next update 30m.
Ethan Park: canary metrics improving: disconnect rate down ~40%, restarts flat. expanding to full us-east.
Hiro Tanaka: PR up for the safer gate: github/redwood#28417.
Ethan Park: full us-east done. will proceed eu-west canary after 10 min steady.
Grace O'Connor: keep timeline updated w/ exact deploy times + link PR.
Vanessa Ortiz: status page posted; shared support brief in #support.

source.content:
Incident Bot: :rotating_light: Incident declared: **Benchmark landing page latency / timeouts** (SEV-2)
Impact: Increased p95 latency + intermittent 5xx on /benchmarks and /benchmarks/data.json
Start: now
IC: @Sean Gallagher
Links: Grafana https://grafana.redwood.ai/d/bench-web | Logs https://logs.redwood.ai/app/bench-results-svc

Sean Gallagher: Taking IC. Can someone confirm scope: marketing site only vs API as well?

PagerDuty: @Sean Gallagher acknowledged **INC-4471** (SEV-2)

Neha Kapoor: Seeing it on the marketing site + results service. Hosted inference API looks normal. CDN cache hit rate fell off a cliff ~5 mins ago.

Ben Carter: Confirmed from marketing side: lots of user reports of “table won’t load / spins forever”. Main landing page HTML sometimes loads, interactive table fails.

Felix Schneider: I’m on it. The table requests `GET /benchmarks/results?model=...&region=...` are timing out. We also have the static JSON export path that the new UI can fall back to.

Sean Gallagher: First actions:
1) stop the bleeding (reduce origin load)
2) restore cache
3) comms to Marketing/Support

Sean Gallagher: @Logan Wright can you check if analytics events are spiking / causing additional requests? Wondering if we introduced extra fetches per filter.

Logan Wright: Looking. Client-side seems to fire a couple events but not enough to explain 10x. However I do see repeated retries from the frontend when fetch > 2s.

Alicia Perez: The UI currently retries 3x on failure with exponential backoff. If origin is slow, that could amplify.

Sean Gallagher: OK, cache miss storm + retries = thundering herd. Let’s focus on cache.

Neha Kapoor: CDN metrics: cache hit rate went from ~92% to ~8%. Origin RPS jumped ~15x. 
There was a config change earlier today to include query params in cache key so filters wouldn’t “stick”. That’s probably it.

Felix Schneider: That was my change in the edge config to fix a bug where `?tab=methodology` showed stale content. I may have over-broadened “cache by query string: all”. :facepalm:

Sean Gallagher: No blame. We revert that now.
@Neha Kapoor can you revert CDN behavior: ignore query string for `/benchmarks/data/*` + `/benchmarks/results*`? Ideally whitelist only `v=` if we need busting.

Neha Kapoor: On it. Proposed:
- `/benchmarks/results*`: cache key = path only (ignore query)
- allow `v=` param only for manual invalidation
- set `stale-while-revalidate=60s` and `stale-if-error=5m`

Mateo Alvarez: FWIW results service CPU is pegged; it’s doing on-demand aggregation when cache is cold. If we can stabilize CDN, it should calm down.

Omar El-Sayed: From tracing: most time is waiting on downstream storage reads (S3) + JSON serialization. Not a runtime issue.

Vanessa Ortiz: Do we need to reserve capacity for the service nodes? I can bump the ASG min if needed.

Sean Gallagher: Hold for 5 mins until CDN change takes effect; then we decide if we need to scale origin.

Wei Chen: Support is pinging docs channel — they’re seeing tweets about “Redwood benchmarks are down”. What’s our approved external line?

Sean Gallagher: For now: “We’re investigating elevated latency on the public benchmark page; inference APIs unaffected.” No ETA yet.

Tyler Benson: Do we want to pause the paid traffic / campaign links?

Ben Carter: If we’re down >10 mins we should pause. I can turn off the launch email resend + ask Growth to pause ads.

Sean Gallagher: Yes please pause paid/extra amplification until stable.

Incident Bot: Update: SEV-2 acknowledged. Next update due in 15 minutes.

Neha Kapoor: CDN config reverted + deployed. Propagation underway. Cache key now ignores query string except `v=`.

Felix Schneider: I can also hotfix frontend to stop retrying on 5xx and switch to the static JSON endpoint if results service is slow.

Sean Gallagher: Do the CDN change first; frontend hotfix if we still see retries causing load. 

Logan Wright: Analytics: pageviews spiked (launch traffic) but nothing abnormal besides repeated `/benchmarks/results` calls from clients when request >2s (likely retry loop).

Neha Kapoor: Cache hit rate is recovering: 8% -> 41% -> 67% over last 3 mins. Origin RPS dropping.

Sean Gallagher: Great. Watch p95.

Vanessa Ortiz: Origin service autoscaler is still scaling up (from 6 -> 14). No harm, but we’ll want to scale back once stable.

Alicia Perez: I tested from home: table loads now, still feels a bit sluggish first load.

Felix Schneider: Same here. First fetch ~1.8s then subsequent filter changes are instant.

Neha Kapoor: p95 for `/benchmarks/results` down from 12s -> 2.4s. 5xx rate trending down.

Sean Gallagher: Nice. Let’s add a mitigation to stop the client retry storm anyway.
@Felix Schneider can you push a quick patch: cap retries to 1 and add circuit breaker (show “results loading slowly, try again” + link to PDF). Also prefer the versioned static JSON if API doesn’t respond in 2s.

Felix Schneider: Yes. I can ship a small change behind a feature flag. ETA 20-30 mins.

Ben Carter: Paid traffic paused. Social posts scheduled for the next hour are on hold. I’ll update PMM.

Wei Chen: I’ll reply to Support with the approved line + that it’s recovering.

Incident Bot: Status update requested by IC.

Sean Gallagher: Current status: Mitigation applied (CDN cache key revert). Service recovering; p95 improving. Next: frontend retry cap + fallback. APIs unaffected.

Neha Kapoor: Confirmed: CDN hit rate back to 89-91%. Origin RPS near baseline +20% (still higher than pre-launch but expected).

Mateo Alvarez: Results service CPU is back under 50%. Queue depth normal.

Sean Gallagher: Great. Let’s keep SEV-2 open until frontend patch is deployed and metrics stable for 15 mins.

PagerDuty: Reminder: Postmortem is required for SEV-2 incidents. Owner: @Sean Gallagher

Felix Schneider: Patch merged + deploying to marketing site now. Changes:
- maxRetries: 1
- timeout: 2s then fallback to `/benchmarks/data/latest.json`
- if still failing, show banner with link to PDF + methodology page

Neha Kapoor: Monitoring: no regression. 5xx now <0.2%.

Alicia Perez: Confirmed banner behavior looks OK. Copy is neutral (no scary language).

Sean Gallagher: Awesome. We can resolve once stable.
Action items (quick):
- add automated check: CDN cache policy for benchmark paths (prevent “cache by all query params”)
- prewarm cache on launch days
- load test interactive page with retries enabled

Incident Bot: :white_check_mark: Incident resolved by @Sean Gallagher. Duration: 1h 3m. 
Resolution summary: Reverted CDN cache key behavior that caused cache miss storm; added client retry cap + fallback to static JSON.

Sean Gallagher: Thanks all. I’ll write the retro notes in eng-sre thread and assign follow-ups. :thumbsup:

Incident Bot: :rotating_light: SEV2 declared: Dedicated pilot customer latency increase during scheduled traffic ramp
Incident Bot: Service: Dedicated Inference (Fleet Autoscaler)
Incident Bot: Affected: acct_hexagon / deployment=hexagon-prod-chat / region=us-east-1
Incident Bot: Commander: Rafael Mendes | Ops: Vanessa Ortiz | Comms: Liam O'Rourke | Scribe: Talia Benson

Rafael Mendes: Seeing pager for p95 latency breach on hexagon-prod-chat. Anyone already looking at graphs?

Allison Grant: I’m in Grafana. p95 went from ~1.6s to 5–7s over last ~20 mins. Queue depth spiking too.
Allison Grant: Dashboard: https://grafana.redwood.internal/d/dedicated-autoscaling/hexagon-prod-chat?from=now-2h&to=now

Liam O'Rourke: Customer pinged TAM channel. They’re in their usual 9am ET ramp and seeing timeouts in app. They asked if we can “pre-scale like yesterday.”

Vanessa Ortiz: Checking autoscaler logs. Desired replicas oscillating: 12 -> 22 -> 14 -> 24 within ~10 min.
Vanessa Ortiz: This is predictive mode enabled (pilot flag). Looks like predictor is swinging up/down.

Keiko Yamamoto: Confirmed in autoscaler loop: `reason=predictive_bias` dominates scale-down decisions even though queue depth is still elevated.
Keiko Yamamoto: It’s respecting bounds (min=12, max=32) but the downscale is too aggressive.

Miles Thornton: Any sign of saturation? GPU util / tokens/sec vs target?

Sergio Costa: GPU util ~55–65% but queue depth is high. That usually means batching config + rampy arrivals. Also error rate is flat; it’s mostly latency/queueing.

Mina Haddad: Routing seeing `capacity_bounded=false`, but `queue_depth` signal is red. If autoscaler is bouncing, routing can’t stabilize. We can temporarily pin to a higher capacity tier / reduce traffic shaping?

Ravi Menon: If we expose “bounded state” to routing it’ll help, but for now quickest is stop the oscillation.

Rafael Mendes: Agree. Immediate mitigation options:
1) disable predictive mode for this deployment
2) manual scale to get ahead of queue
3) increase min temporarily until ramp passes

Vanessa Ortiz: I can flip predictive off via feature flag + policy patch. We should also do a one-time scale up to 26 to drain queue.

Lauren Bishop: +1 disable predictive for Hexagon now. We can’t have pilot impacting their business hours. We’ll follow up w/ postmortem + next steps.

Talia Benson: Capturing: decision proposal = disable predictive mode temporarily for hexagon-prod-chat (pilot) and fall back to reactive scaling; manually scale up to drain queue.

Mei Lin: Looking at predictor service. Forecast updated ~6 min ago, but it’s bouncing because it’s overfitting the last 15 min window. Confidence is low but still emitting a value.
Mei Lin: We should treat low confidence as “no prediction” (fallback to reactive) until we tighten gating.

Sana Farid: Prediction error is large right now (actual demand > predicted after the initial spike). Also the derivative is noisy. +1 to “confidence threshold” gating.

Rafael Mendes: Ok, execute mitigation.
Vanessa can you disable predictive for this deployment and do the scale up? Keiko, can you confirm cooldown/dampening isn’t misconfigured?

Keiko Yamamoto: Checking policy config from control plane. cooldown_up=60s cooldown_down=60s, predictive_bias_strength=0.7 (pretty high). That combo + noisy forecast explains oscillation.

Vanessa Ortiz: On it. Applying policy patch:
- predictive_mode: false
- keep bounds unchanged
Also sending one-time override to 26 replicas.

Deploy Bot: Applying scaling policy update (deployment=hexagon-prod-chat region=us-east-1) requested_by=vanessa.ortiz
Deploy Bot: Patch: predictive_mode=false

Deploy Bot: One-time capacity override: desired_replicas=26 (expires in 45m)

Allison Grant: Watching graphs. Desired replicas just snapped to 26. Queue depth peaked ~4.8k, starting to come down.
Allison Grant: Latency p95 trending down from 6.8s -> 4.9s.

Liam O'Rourke: I’ll message customer: “we’ve disabled predictive scaling and increased capacity; expect latency to improve over next 10–15 min; will follow up with RCA.”

Miles Thornton: Make sure we don’t violate their cost expectations. Are we still within their max bound? (max=32 so ok). We should mention this is within the agreed ceiling.

Vanessa Ortiz: Yep within max=32. Reactive should ramp up if needed; override is temp.

Rafael Mendes: Great. Next: confirm scale-down safety. We should prevent dropping below safe baseline during ramp.

Sergio Costa: For this incident, alert was triggered by `dedicated_queue_depth_p95` + `latency_p95` for 10m. After predictive off, the `scale_event_rate` is stable.

Keiko Yamamoto: Suggest we also set cooldown_down to 300s for Hexagon for the rest of the day to avoid sharp downscales.

Lauren Bishop: Please do, but keep it minimal. We should avoid config churn unless needed.

Vanessa Ortiz: I’d prefer to keep policy changes to just disabling predictive for now, and let the override expire. If oscillation returns under reactive, we can patch cooldowns.

Rafael Mendes: Agree. Let’s keep changes minimal during incident.

Allison Grant: Update: queue depth down to ~1.9k. p95 now ~2.7s and improving. GPU util up to ~72% (better). No error spike.

Ravi Menon: Routing stable. No additional fallbacks triggered.

Talia Benson: Timeline note:
- T0 (thread start): p95 6–7s, queue depth ~4–5k, capacity oscillating
- T+12m: predictive disabled + override to 26
- T+20m: p95 down to ~3s, queue draining

Mei Lin: Action item: add gating so predictor outputs with low confidence are treated as null and autoscaler falls back to reactive (and never triggers scale-down). Also review windowing.

Sana Farid: + add metric/alert for “prediction confidence low but applied” so we catch earlier.

Rafael Mendes: We’ll open follow-ups after mitigation holds. For now keep incident open until latency back within SLO for 30m.

Allison Grant: p95 now 1.9s, back within Hexagon’s target. Queue depth ~450 and falling.

Liam O'Rourke: Customer confirms improvement, timeouts stopped. They asked if predictive will be re-enabled later; I told them we’ll keep it off pending fixes and schedule a check-in.

Miles Thornton: Keep note: incident root likely predictive downscale bias + short cooldown + noisy forecast during ramp.

Vanessa Ortiz: Override expires in ~25m. Reactive desired is hovering 24–26 so shouldn’t drop suddenly.

Incident Bot: Status update requested by Commander

Rafael Mendes: Status: Mitigated. Disabled predictive scaling for hexagon-prod-chat and applied temporary scale-up override to drain queue. Latency/queue depth back within SLO; monitoring until ramp completes. Next steps: file bug for predictor confidence gating + autoscaler scale-down constraints.

Talia Benson: Follow-up tickets to file:
- Bug: predictive confidence gating / treat low-confidence as null
- Bug: predictive must not drive scale-down when reactive signals indicate queue buildup
- Add runbook note for “disable predictive + temp override” as first mitigation

Keiko Yamamoto: I’ll file the autoscaler constraint issue and link logs.

Mei Lin: I’ll file predictor gating + evaluation changes.

Allison Grant: I’ll add a panel on dashboard for prediction confidence vs applied bias (if not already). Also can add temporary annotation for this event.

Rafael Mendes: Marking resolved once we hold steady 30m.

Allison Grant: 30m stable. p95 ~1.7s, queue depth <100.

Incident Bot: :white_check_mark: SEV2 resolved by Rafael Mendes
Incident Bot: Resolution summary: Predictive mode disabled for hexagon-prod-chat; temporary scale-up override applied; service returned to SLO.
Incident Bot: Reminder: post-incident review due within 3 business days.

source.messages:
Incident Bot: :warning: Near-miss declared (no customer-facing SEV) — elevated KMS timeouts + retry amplification observed in us-east. Tracking here for summary + follow-ups.

Sean Gallagher: Heads up: we had a near-miss ~10 mins ago where KMS started timing out and the platform amplified load via retries. We didn’t fully tip over, but we got uncomfortably close (5xx spiked + queue depth climbing). Posting details as we learn more.

Allison Grant: Do we have a dashboard snapshot? Want to capture before things normalize.

Sean Gallagher: Yep. Key panels:
- KMS p95 latency jumped from ~40ms -> 2-3s.
- `kms.requests` doubled within ~2 mins.
- `kms.errors{code=Timeout}` sustained at ~8-12% for ~6 mins.
- API 5xx went from baseline <0.2% -> peaked ~2.1% then recovered.
Likely retry storm: clients doing 3 retries *and* some upstream services have their own retry wrappers.

Rafael Mendes: Confirming: KMS dependency looked degraded, not hard down. We saw request volume jump in lockstep with timeout rate.

Ethan Park: Which call sites? control-plane only or data-plane too?

Sean Gallagher: Both. Data-plane tokenization path was fine, but envelope decrypt path hit KMS heavily when cache miss rate increased. Control-plane was also calling KMS for a config decrypt loop (see traces).

Logan Wright: Pulling traces now. Seeing spans like `kms.decrypt` nested under `secrets.resolve` + retries at multiple layers. We’re basically doing exponential backoff without jitter in a few places.

Benji Okafor: We should assume double-retry: service A retries, wrapper retries, then client retries. That’s ~9 attempts under load.

Tomáš Novák: Any sign of circuit breaker opening? Or are we just hammering until recovery?

Sean Gallagher: Circuit breaker didn’t trip because thresholds are on error rate but timeouts were being counted inconsistently (some mapped to “unknown”). Also, timeouts were returning late enough that the breaker window wasn’t seeing them as failures fast enough.

Allison Grant: That “timeouts mapped inconsistently” is a big deal. We need canonical error taxonomy enforcement. (ENG-4822 vibes)

Kevin Osei: +1. Also we need to log retry counts and final outcome consistently. Right now the on-call view is “KMS slow” but not “we retried 8x and multiplied traffic.”

Marcus Lin: From a security standpoint: we should be careful with any “fail-open” behavior. But for *timeouts*, we can and should reduce amplification and provide backpressure. Also, any caching changes need to respect rotation safety.

Rafael Mendes: Immediate mitigation we took:
- Temporarily reduced retry count on the data-plane service via config override (2 -> 0) for the hot path.
- Lowered concurrency for the KMS call worker pool.
After that, KMS volume dropped and error rate recovered.

Connor O'Brien: Was there a deploy preceding the spike or purely external (AWS KMS)?

Sean Gallagher: No deploy in the last ~45 mins for the impacted cluster. Looks like upstream KMS latency degradation + internal retry behavior.

Nadia Rahman: I checked metrics: the retry attempt histogram isn’t emitted for all clients. Some emit `attempt=1..n`, some don’t tag attempts at all. We should standardize.

Logan Wright: Root cause hypothesis:
1) KMS started timing out intermittently.
2) Our wrapper retries with backoff but little/no jitter.
3) Upstream also retries (grpc client + app-level), causing multiplicative load.
4) Increased KMS latency causes more in-flight, which raises timeout probability => feedback loop.

Ethan Park: Proposed immediate fix: cap retry budget globally + add jitter. Also enforce “only one layer retries” (wrapper owns it; upstream should not).

Benji Okafor: We can ship a hotfix in the wrapper to:
- default retries to 1 (or 0) for decrypt in prod
- add full jitter
- add a hard cap on total time spent retrying (`retry_budget_ms`)
- classify timeout errors correctly so circuit breaker can open

Allison Grant: + alerting side: we should add an alert on “KMS request multiplier” (e.g., requests per app request) or at least on retry rate. If KMS volume grows faster than app QPS, page.

Sean Gallagher: Agree. For tonight, main goal is preventing recurrence. Benji/Ethan can you own a hotfix PR? Link it here.

Benji Okafor: On it. Will target `redwood` monorepo wrapper. Draft PR in ~30.

Incident Bot: :memo: Reminder — add incident summary + action items before closing the near-miss.

Rafael Mendes: Also: we should document this in the KMS decrypt failures runbook as a distinct failure mode: “retry amplification / storm.” Current runbook focuses on AccessDenied + invalid ciphertext.

Kevin Osei: For compliance/audit: this is operational reliability, but we should note any change in behavior that affects decrypt availability. Make sure the change is “retry policy only,” no crypto semantics.

Marcus Lin: Please ensure we don’t end up caching decrypt results in a way that violates key rotation expectations. But reducing retries is fine.

Benji Okafor: PR up: `redwood` pr-18563 “hotfix: reduce KMS retry storm on timeout”
- retries: 3 -> 1 default for decrypt ops
- adds jitter to backoff
- adds `max_total_retry_ms` = 800ms default
- maps timeout errors to canonical `KMS_TIMEOUT`
- emits `kms.retry_attempts` + `kms.retry_budget_exhausted` counters
Requesting SRE review.

Sean Gallagher: Looking now.

Allison Grant: :eyes: checking metric naming — please align with existing namespace (`dep.kms.*` vs `kms.*`). We don’t want another round of inconsistent dashboards.

Logan Wright: I left comments on PR re: trace spans — include `retry_count` + `retry_budget_ms` attributes on final span.

Connor O'Brien: If we ship hotfix, what’s rollout plan? Canary first in us-east only? Need go/no-go criteria.

Sean Gallagher: Rollout proposal:
1) Canary 5% traffic in us-east-1, watch 30 mins.
2) Expand to 25%, watch 30 mins.
3) Full us-east-1, then other regions.
Go/no-go:
- KMS timeout rate stable or improved
- No increase in customer-visible decrypt failures (watch 5xx + `decrypt_failed`)
- Latency p95 for affected endpoints does not regress >5%
Rollback trigger:
- `decrypt_failed` > 0.5% sustained 5 mins or any “invalid ciphertext” anomaly.

Rafael Mendes: Also set temporary alert: page if `KMS_TIMEOUT` > 2% and `kms.requests` slope > app QPS slope. We can tune later.

Allison Grant: +1, but please route to low-urgency page for now. This is still rollout.

Tomáš Novák: Quick question: if we reduce retries, will we increase user-facing failures during brief KMS blips?

Sean Gallagher: Yes, potentially. But current behavior is worse: it can take the whole system down. Better to fail fast + rely on circuit breaker + backpressure, and (where possible) serve from cache for reads that allow it.

Ethan Park: For control-plane paths we can often tolerate transient failure and retry at *workflow* level (not per-request) with queueing. For hot request paths, we should be conservative.

Kevin Osei: We should add a follow-up task to ensure “retry policy by operation” is explicit (decrypt vs encrypt vs describeKey). Audit folks sometimes ask for “availability controls,” but we can justify it as reliability.

Sean Gallagher: PR approved from SRE side with minor nits. Ship it.

Connor O'Brien: Deploy bot says canary started in us-east-1.

Incident Bot: Deploy Bot: `redwood-api` canary rollout initiated (us-east-1) — build from pr-18563.

Nadia Rahman: Watching dashboards: KMS request volume normalized. Retry attempt metric now showing distribution (mostly 0-1). Good sign.

Allison Grant: Timeout mapping looks fixed — error code breakdown now has `KMS_TIMEOUT` instead of “unknown.” Circuit breaker panel finally meaningful.

Rafael Mendes: During canary: KMS p95 still elevated (~900ms) but app 5xx not moving. Looks stable.

Sean Gallagher: Great. Proceed to 25%.

Incident Bot: Deploy Bot: rollout expanded to 25% (us-east-1).

Marcus Lin: Please make sure we capture a short writeup for the rotation/hardening initiative — this is exactly why we need the wrapper standard + guardrails.

Ethan Park: Follow-ups I’m capturing:
- Ensure only wrapper handles retries (remove upstream retries) — audit call sites.
- Add config guardrails to prevent “3 retries + long timeout” in prod.
- Add “retry storm” section to runbook + game day scenario.

Sean Gallagher: Adding action items explicitly:
1) (Benji/Ethan) Backport hotfix to remaining services using old KMS client in next 48h.
2) (Allison/Nadia) Standardize metric names/tags for `kms.*` and retry attempt fields.
3) (Tomáš/Rafael) Update on-call runbook: symptoms + mitigations for retry storm; include config override steps.
4) (Logan) Add tracing attributes + sample query for “retry amplification.”
5) (Kevin/Marcus) Update ADR-007 notes / wrapper policy doc: single-layer retry + budgets.

Connor O'Brien: Rollout completed in us-east-1. No red flags.

Incident Bot: :white_check_mark: Near-miss resolved. Please add final summary for record.

Sean Gallagher: Final summary:
- What happened: Upstream KMS latency/timeout degradation led to amplified KMS traffic due to multi-layer retries (wrapper + client + upstream), causing elevated 5xx and queue growth.
- Impact: brief 5xx spike (~2.1% peak) in us-east-1; recovered without customer escalation; classified as near-miss.
- Mitigation: temporarily disabled retries + reduced worker concurrency; shipped hotfix PR 18563 to cap retries, add jitter, enforce retry budget, and map timeout errors consistently.
- Status: hotfix rolled out in us-east-1 successfully; plan to roll to other regions tomorrow with same canary gates.
- Follow-ups: see action list above (runbook update, telemetry standardization, remove duplicate retries, guardrails).

Rafael Mendes: :thumbsup: I’ll schedule a quick retro slot this week to fold into the KMS hardening epic + make sure game day includes “retry amplification” scenario.

title: #incidents: 1747779033
preview:
Incident Bot: :warning: Near-miss declared (no customer-facing SEV) — elevated KMS timeouts + retry amplification observed in us-east. Tracking here for summary + follow-ups.

Sean Gallagher: Heads up: we had a near-miss ~10 mins ago where KMS started timing out and the platform amplified load via retries. We didn’t fully tip over, but we got uncomfortably close (5xx spiked + queue depth climbing). Posting details as we learn more.

Allison Grant: Do we have a dashboard snapshot? Want to capture before things normalize.

Sean Gallagher: Yep. Key panels:
- KMS p95 latency jumped from ~40ms -> 2-3s.
- `kms.requests` doubled within ~2 mins.
- `kms.errors{code=Timeout}` sustained at ~8-12% for ~6 mins.
- API 5xx went from baseline <0.2% -> peaked ~2.1% then recovered.
Likely retry storm: clients doing 3 retries *and* some upstream services have their own retry wrappers.

Rafael Mendes: Confirming: KMS dependency looked degraded, not hard down. We saw request volume jump in lockstep with timeout rate.

Ethan Park: Which call sites? control-plane only or data-plane too?

Sean Gallagher: Both. Data-plane tokenization path was fine, but envelope decrypt path hit KMS heavily when cache miss rate increased. Control-plane was also calling KMS for a config decrypt loop (see traces).

Logan Wright: Pulling traces now. Seeing spans like `kms.decrypt` nested under `secrets.resolve` + retries at multiple layers. We’re basically doing exponential backoff without jitter in a few places.

Benji Okafor: We should assume double-retry: service A retries, wrapper retries, then client retries. That’s ~9 attempts under load.

Tomáš Novák: Any sign of circuit breaker opening? Or are we just hammering until recovery?

Sean Gallagher: Circuit breaker didn’t trip because thresholds are on error rate but timeouts were being counted inconsistently (some mapped to “unknown”). Also, timeouts were returning late enough that the breaker window wasn’t seeing them as failures fast enough.

Allison Grant: That “timeouts mapped inconsistently” is a big deal. We need canonical error taxonomy enforcement. (ENG-4822 vibes)

Kevin Osei: +1. Also we need to log retry counts and final outcome consistently. Right now the on-call view is “KMS slow” but not “we retried 8x and multiplied traffic.”

Marcus Lin: From a security standpoint: we should be careful with any “fail-open” behavior. But for *timeouts*, we can and should reduce amplification and provide backpressure. Also, any caching changes need to respect rotation safety.

Rafael Mendes: Immediate mitigation we took:
- Temporarily reduced retry count on the data-plane service via config override (2 -> 0) for the hot path.
- Lowered concurrency for the KMS call worker pool.
After that, KMS volume dropped and error rate recovered.

Connor O'Brien: Was there a deploy preceding the spike or purely external (AWS KMS)?

Sean Gallagher: No deploy in the last ~45 mins for the impacted cluster. Looks like upstream KMS latency degradation + internal retry behavior.

Nadia Rahman: I checked metrics: the retry attempt histogram isn’t emitted for all clients. Some emit `attempt=1..n`, some don’t tag attempts at all. We should standardize.

Logan Wright: Root cause hypothesis:
1) KMS started timing out intermittently.
2) Our wrapper retries with backoff but little/no jitter.
3) Upstream also retries (grpc client + app-level), causing multiplicative load.
4) Increased KMS latency causes more in-flight, which raises timeout probability => feedback loop.

Ethan Park: Proposed immediate fix: cap retry budget globally + add jitter. Also enforce “only one layer retries” (wrapper owns it; upstream should not).

Benji Okafor: We can ship a hotfix in the wrapper to:
- default retries to 1 (or 0) for decrypt in prod
- add full jitter
- add a hard cap on total time spent retrying (`retry_budget_ms`)
- classify timeout errors correctly so circuit breaker can open

Allison Grant: + alerting side: we should add an alert on “KMS request multiplier” (e.g., requests per app request) or at least on retry rate. If KMS volume grows faster than app QPS, page.

Sean Gallagher: Agree. For tonight, main goal is preventing recurrence. Benji/Ethan can you own a hotfix PR? Link it here.

Benji Okafor: On it. Will target `redwood` monorepo wrapper. Draft PR in ~30.

Incident Bot: :memo: Reminder — add incident summary + action items before closing the near-miss.

Rafael Mendes: Also: we should document this in the KMS decrypt failures runbook as a distinct failure mode: “retry amplification / storm.” Current runbook focuses on AccessDenied + invalid ciphertext.

Kevin Osei: For compliance/audit: this is operational reliability, but we should note any change in behavior that affects decrypt availability. Make sure the change is “retry policy only,” no crypto semantics.

Marcus Lin: Please ensure we don’t end up caching decrypt results in a way that violates key rotation expectations. But reducing retries is fine.

Benji Okafor: PR up: `redwood` pr-18563 “hotfix: reduce KMS retry storm on timeout”

- adds jitter to backoff
- adds `max_total_retry_ms` = 800ms default
- maps timeout errors to canonical `KMS_TIMEOUT`
- emits `kms.retry_attempts` + `kms.retry_budget_exhausted` counters
Requesting SRE review.

Sean Gallagher: Looking now.

Allison Grant: :eyes: checking metric naming — please align with existing namespace (`dep.kms.*` vs `kms.*`). We don’t want another round of inconsistent dashboards.

Logan Wright: I left comments on PR re: trace spans — include `retry_count` + `retry_budget_ms` attributes on final span.

Connor O'Brien: If we ship hotfix, what’s rollout plan? Canary first in us-east only? Need go/no-go criteria.

Sean Gallagher: Rollout proposal:
1) Canary 5% traffic in us-east-1, watch 30 mins.
2) Expand to 25%, watch 30 mins.
3) Full us-east-1, then other regions.
Go/no-go:
- KMS timeout rate stable or improved
- No increase in customer-visible decrypt failures (watch 5xx + `decrypt_failed`)
- Latency p95 for affected endpoints does not regress >5%
Rollback trigger:
- `decrypt_failed` > 0.5% sustained 5 mins or any “invalid ciphertext” anomaly.

Rafael Mendes: Also set temporary alert: page if `KMS_TIMEOUT` > 2% and `kms.requests` slope > app QPS slope. We can tune later.

source.messages:
Incident Bot: :rotating_light: Incident opened: **Private Prometheus scrape failure after NetworkPolicy change**
Severity: SEV-2 (customer impact)
Customer: (redacted) Private VPC install
Signal: Prometheus targets down / dashboards blank
Owner: @Rafael Mendes

Rafael Mendes: seeing report from Support that all Redwood dashboards went empty ~30 min ago. Likely Prometheus isn’t scraping our pods.
Can someone confirm if this lines up with any recent change in the customer cluster? @Owen Phillips

Owen Phillips: yep. Customer did a “security hardening” pass and tightened NetworkPolicies this morning. After that, Grafana shows “No data” and Prometheus /targets is mostly DOWN for `redwood-*` jobs.

Sean Gallagher: got it. classic. if they went default-deny in namespaces, Prometheus can’t reach pod `:metrics` ports.
Do we know how Prometheus is deployed in their setup? In our min pack it’s `prometheus` in `observability` ns. Some customers run their own in `monitoring`.

Bruno Silva: pulling their install notes from SUP ticket — they’re using our bundled kube-prometheus-stack, namespace is `redwood-observability`. Prometheus pod label `app.kubernetes.io/name=prometheus`.

Diego Santos: can we get the failing target details? usually it’s connection timeout (network policy) vs 403 (auth) vs TLS mismatch.

Owen Phillips: from customer screenshot of /targets:
- `redwood-runtime` endpoints: `context deadline exceeded`
- `redwood-control-plane` endpoints: same
No 401/403.

Rafael Mendes: ok. so scrape requests can’t connect.
We should ask them to run:
`kubectl -n redwood-observability exec -it deploy/prometheus-kube-prometheus-prometheus -- wget -S -O- http://<podIP>:<metricsPort>/metrics`
But podIP is annoying. Better: `kubectl -n redwood-runtime get endpoints` and hit service.

Sean Gallagher: +1. simplest check:
```
kubectl -n redwood-observability exec -it deploy/prometheus-kube-prometheus-prometheus -- sh
# inside:
apk add --no-cache curl
curl -v http://redwood-runtime-metrics.redwood-runtime.svc:9090/metrics
```
(or whatever svc/port).
If that times out -> definitely NP.

Allison Grant: reminder: our default metrics service is `*-metrics` w/ port name `metrics`. Prometheus discovers via ServiceMonitor selecting `release=prometheus` label. NP breaks traffic even if SM is correct.

Tomáš Novák: do we have a canned NetworkPolicy snippet for “allow Prometheus to scrape all Redwood namespaces”? We should, because this will recur.

Diego Santos: we don’t ship one today (we rely on “don’t block intra-cluster” assumption). But we can provide recommended NP in runbook.
For immediate: allow ingress to pods on metrics ports from Prometheus namespace/podSelector.

Rafael Mendes: action plan:
1) confirm it’s NP by curling from Prometheus pod
2) if yes, provide NP example for customer to apply
3) verify Prometheus targets recover

Rafael Mendes: @Bruno Silva can you ask customer to run the curl test from Prom pod and paste output? Also ask what NPs they applied (namespace-wide default deny?)

Bruno Silva: on it.

Sean Gallagher: meanwhile drafting NP example. We need to cover multiple namespaces: `redwood-runtime`, `redwood-control-plane`, `redwood-installer` maybe.
Assuming customer uses default-deny ingress in those namespaces, we can add “allow from prom” policy scoped to pods with `redwood.ai/metrics=true` or the metrics port.
But if they did default-deny egress on Prometheus ns, we need allow egress too.

Allison Grant: + we should keep cardinality labels out of this :)
But yes, allow from Prometheus pods to metrics ports only.

Sahana Iyer: FYI smoke tests in our harness don’t include default-deny NP scenarios yet. This is a good candidate for adding to the pack validation suite.

Rafael Mendes: agreed. add follow-up.

Bruno Silva: customer response:
- They added default-deny ingress in `redwood-runtime` and `redwood-control-plane` namespaces.
- They also restricted cross-namespace traffic to “only needed ports”.
Curl from Prometheus pod:
